symbols: diagonal cross (saltire) — the instrument of Andrew's martyrdom, the X-shaped cross on which he was crucified
upward gaze into the light — the martyr's vision of heavenly glory in the moment of suffering
muscular aged body — the strength of faith persisting through age and trial
beam of light from above — divine grace illuminating the saint in his final witness
description: A powerful half-length figure of an elderly, muscular man with white hair and beard, shown bare-chested against a dark background. He gazes upward and to the left where a beam of light enters diagonally. His right hand rests on his chest while his left arm extends outward. Behind him, the dark diagonal form of a large X-shaped cross is barely visible. The dramatic lighting illuminates his weathered face and sinewy arms against the deep darkness, creating an intensely physical and spiritual portrait.
theological_description: Saint Andrew, brother of Peter and one of the first called by Jesus (John 1:40-42), was traditionally martyred on an X-shaped cross in Patras, Greece. According to tradition, Andrew considered himself unworthy to be crucified on the same type of cross as his Lord and requested the diagonal form. His upward gaze in this portrait captures the moment described in hagiographic sources: as Andrew was bound to the cross, he preached for two days before dying, and witnesses reported that a heavenly light surrounded him. Andrew's martyrdom completes the pattern Jesus established when He called him: 'Follow me' (Matthew 4:19) — a following that led from the Sea of Galilee to Golgotha's shadow. The raw physicality of the depiction affirms that Christian martyrdom involves real bodies and real suffering, not merely spiritual ideals.

scripture_refs.reason: Andrew as one of the first disciples called, who then brought his brother Peter to Jesus
Jesus calling Andrew and Peter from their fishing nets — the beginning of the journey that ends in martyrdom
Where I am, my servant will be also — Andrew following Christ even unto death on a cross
I have fought the good fight — the apostle's completion of his race
